answer: From Sumerian to Early Babylonian, the script shifted from detailed pictograms to wedge-impressed signs with reduced strokes and standardization. From Early Babylonian to Late Babylonian, glyphs simplified further, becoming more linear and cursive with fewer variants. From Late Babylonian to Assyrian, the script retained much similarity but developed more angular wedges and regional adaptations for faster writing.
